title: Functions & Transformations
blurb: Function notation as a machine, domain and range, the four graph moves — shift, flip, stretch — plus absolute value and piecewise functions: the visual grammar every later unit speaks.
buildsOn: Algebra 1's functions unit — f(x) notation and linear graphs.

lessons.title: Function Notation, Domain & Range
lessons.concreteComparison: A vending machine takes a button press and returns exactly one snack — same button, same snack, every time. A function is that machine for numbers: input $x$, one guaranteed output $f(x)$. Domain is the list of buttons that work; range is the set of snacks it can actually drop.
lessons.objective: Evaluate functions fluently (numbers, expressions, and nested calls), and read domain and range restrictions from formulas.
concept: $f(x) = 2x^2 - 3x + 1$ names a rule. $f(4)$ means 'run 4 through it': $2(16) - 12 + 1 = 21$. Inputs can be expressions too: $f(a + 1)$ substitutes the WHOLE package, parentheses and all: $2(a+1)^2 - 3(a+1) + 1$.

**Domain** = every input the formula tolerates. Two classic outlaws: division by zero ($\frac{1}{x-3}$ bans $x = 3$) and even roots of negatives ($\sqrt{x-5}$ demands $x \ge 5$). Everything else is welcome.
**Range** = every output the machine can actually produce. $f(x) = x^2$ never goes negative — its range is $y \ge 0$ — even though its domain is all reals. Domain reads inputs; range reads outputs.
lessons.keyIdea: f(x) is a machine: substitute the whole input, and read domain from what the formula forbids (zero denominators, negative even roots).

facts.title: Substitute the package
facts.explanation: Parentheses around the whole input — the most-skipped step in algebra.

facts.title: Domain outlaws
facts.explanation: Ban zero denominators; even roots need non-negative insides.

workedExamples.solution: $f(4) = 32 - 12 + 1 = 21$. $f(-2) = 8 + 6 + 1 = 15$. $f(a+1) = 2(a+1)^2 - 3(a+1) + 1 = 2a^2 + a$ after expanding — the whole package substitutes.
$g$: the denominator $x^2 - 9 = (x-3)(x+3)$ dies at $x = \pm 3$ — domain: all reals except $3$ and $-3$. $h$: need $2x - 8 \ge 0$, so $x \ge 4$.

commonMistakes.correction: $f(a+1) \ne f(a) + 1$. The input is the package $(a+1)$: every $x$ in the formula becomes $(a+1)$, parentheses included.
Zero under a square root is fine: $\sqrt{0} = 0$. The condition is $x - 5 \ge 0$, so $x \ge 5$ — the boundary belongs.

tryIt.solution: Root: $x + 2 \ge 0 \to x \ge -2$. Denominator: $x \ne 5$. Both at once: $x \ge -2$ and $x \ne 5$.

steps.title: One rule, a thousand questions
steps.body: Algebra 2 is a course about FUNCTIONS — quadratics, polynomials, exponentials, logarithms, rationals. Every one of them is the same object underneath: a machine that takes an input and returns exactly one output. Get fluent with the machine now and every later unit is just a new engine in the same chassis.

steps.title: Run the machine
steps.teach: This is $f(x) = 2x + 3$ as a live machine. Slide the input and watch the output respond — one input, one output, every time. THAT reliability is what makes $f$ a function; the graph is just every (input, output) pair plotted at once.

steps.title: Substitute the package
steps.body: $f(4)$: replace every $x$ with 4. $f(-2)$: replace every $x$ with $(-2)$ — parentheses shield the sign. $f(a+1)$: replace every $x$ with the ENTIRE package $(a+1)$. Wherever the formula squares $x$, it now squares the package. Writing the parentheses first and simplifying second prevents nearly every substitution error.

steps.title: The package rule
steps.explanation: $(a+3)^2 + 1 = a^2 + 6a + 9 + 1 = a^2 + 6a + 10$. The middle term $6a$ is exactly what dropping the parentheses loses.

steps.title: What the formula forbids
steps.body: Most formulas accept every real number — the interesting ones don't. Two outlaws to hunt: a **denominator hitting zero** (ban those inputs) and an **even root of a negative** (demand the inside stay $\ge 0$). State the domain as what's allowed: 'all reals except 3', or '$x \ge 4$'.

steps.title: Spot the outlaws
steps.explanation: The root demands $x - 1 \ge 0$; the DENOMINATOR bans the root being zero, killing $x = 1$ too. Together: strictly $x > 1$.

steps.title: What comes out
steps.body: Range is the output side: which heights does the graph actually reach? $f(x) = x^2$ has domain all reals but range $y \ge 0$ — squares are never negative. Range usually reads best from the GRAPH: lowest point, highest point, gaps. This course builds that graph-reading eye function family by family.

steps.title: Domain before algebra
steps.body: Before manipulating any formula, note its domain restrictions in the margin. Later — solving rational and radical equations — phantom 'solutions' will appear precisely at forbidden inputs, and the margin note is what catches them.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $f(\text{input})$: substitute the whole package, parentheses first.
Domain outlaws: zero denominators, negative insides of even roots.
Domain reads inputs from the formula; range reads outputs from the graph.

lessons.title: Transformations of Functions
lessons.concreteComparison: Photo apps don't redraw your picture pixel by pixel — they slide it, flip it, stretch it. Algebra does exactly this to graphs: four standard moves turn one memorized parent shape into a whole family, and the formula tells you the recipe.
lessons.objective: Read h, k, a in g(x) = a·f(x − h) + k as graph moves: horizontal/vertical shifts, reflection, and vertical stretch/compression.
concept: **Shifts**: $f(x) + k$ slides the graph UP by $k$ (outside = vertical). $f(x - h)$ slides it RIGHT by $h$ (inside = horizontal, and the sign lies: $x - 3$ moves right, $x + 3$ moves left).

**Reflection and stretch**: $-f(x)$ flips over the x-axis. $a \cdot f(x)$ stretches vertically by $a$ when $|a| > 1$, compresses when $|a| < 1$. Applied to $y = x^2$: $y = -2(x-3)^2 + 5$ is the parabola flipped, doubled, moved right 3 and up 5 — vertex now $(3, 5)$.

Order matters only between stretch/flip and vertical shift: $a f(x-h) + k$ means stretch/flip FIRST, then shift. Reading order: inside moves (horizontal), then $a$ (shape), then outside $+ k$ (vertical).
lessons.keyIdea: Inside the parentheses moves the graph horizontally (sign reversed); outside moves it vertically; a out front flips and stretches — one parent shape, four dials.

facts.title: The transformation recipe
facts.explanation: $h$: right-shift (sign lies), $k$: up-shift, $a$: flip if negative, stretch if $|a|>1$.

facts.title: Inside vs outside
facts.explanation: Inside touches the input (horizontal); outside touches the output (vertical).

workedExamples.statement: Describe $g(x) = (x + 4)^2 - 3$ as transformations of $y = x^2$, and give its vertex.
workedExamples.solution: Inside: $x + 4 = x - (-4)$ → LEFT 4. Outside: $-3$ → DOWN 3. No flip or stretch. The parent vertex $(0,0)$ lands at $(-4, -3)$. Check a point: $g(-4) = 0 - 3 = -3$ ✓, and $g(-2) = 4 - 3 = 1$.

workedExamples.statement: The graph of $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ is flipped over the x-axis, stretched vertically by 2, and shifted right 1 and up 6. Write $g(x)$ and find where it crosses the x-axis.

commonMistakes.correction: Inside signs lie: $x + 3 = x - (-3)$ shifts LEFT 3. Test one point: the input that used to give $f(0)$ is now $x = -3$.

commonMistakes.text: Applying the vertical shift before the stretch in $y = 2f(x) + 1$.
commonMistakes.correction: Stretch first, then shift: a point at height 3 goes to $2(3) + 1 = 7$, not $2(3 + 1) = 8$. The formula's order of operations IS the transformation order.

tryIt.statement: Give the vertex of $g(x) = -3(x - 2)^2 + 8$ and say whether it opens up or down.
tryIt.solution: Vertex $(2, 8)$ — inside gives right 2, outside up 8. $a = -3 < 0$: opens DOWN (flipped), three times steeper. Check: $g(2) = 8$, $g(3) = -3 + 8 = 5$.

tryIt.statement: Write the equation of $y = |x|$ shifted left 5 and down 2, and find its two x-intercepts.

steps.title: One shape, a whole family
steps.body: You know $y = x^2$ cold. But real problems hand you $y = -2(x-3)^2 + 5$. Redraw from a table? Never. Algebra 2's answer: memorize ONE parent shape per family, then learn the four moves — shift horizontally, shift vertically, flip, stretch — that the formula encodes. Same four moves for every family, all course long.

steps.title: Drive the vertex
steps.teach: This is $y = a(x - h)^2 + k$ with live dials. Step $h$ and watch the parabola slide HORIZONTALLY — notice the formula shows $x - 3$ when the graph sits at $+3$: inside signs lie. Step $k$ for vertical slides. The vertex is always exactly $(h, k)$.

steps.title: Inside lies, outside doesn't
steps.body: OUTSIDE the function touches the output: $f(x) + 3$ lifts every point 3 — honest. INSIDE touches the input: $f(x - 3)$ asks 'which x now gives the old $f(0)$?' Answer: $x = 3$ — so the graph moves RIGHT even though the formula says minus. Inside: horizontal, sign reversed. Every family, forever.

steps.title: Which way?
steps.prompt: The graph of $y = f(x + 2) - 7$ is the graph of $f$ moved:
steps.explanation: Inside: $x + 2 = x - (-2)$ → left 2 (the sign lies). Outside: $-7$ → down 7 (honest).

steps.title: The a dial: flip and stretch
steps.teach: Now step $a$. Past 1 the parabola narrows (every output stretched); between 0 and 1 it flattens; below 0 it FLIPS into an arch. $a$ never moves the vertex — it only reshapes around it. Shape dial and position dials are independent.

steps.title: Stretch, then lift
steps.explanation: Formula order: multiply first, $3 \cdot 5 = 15$, then subtract 4: $g(2) = 11$. Stretch before shift, always.

steps.title: Track the landmark
steps.body: Don't transform clouds of points — transform ONE landmark. For parabolas it's the vertex; for $\sqrt{x}$ the endpoint; for $|x|$ the corner. Move the landmark with $(h, k)$, apply $a$ for shape, sketch. Three seconds, no table.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Stretch before shift; move the landmark, not the point cloud.

lessons.title: Absolute Value Functions & Equations
lessons.concreteComparison: "We're within 3 degrees of freezing" names TWO temperatures at once — 3 above and 3 below. Absolute value is the algebra of that sentence: distance from a center, direction ignored, and its graph is the sharpest shape in the course: a perfect V.
lessons.objective: Graph absolute value functions via transformations, and solve absolute value equations and inequalities by the two-branch method.
concept: $|x|$ is DISTANCE from zero: $|5| = |-5| = 5$. The graph of $y = |x|$ is a V with its corner at the origin — two rays of slope $\pm 1$. The full family $y = a|x - h| + k$ moves the corner to $(h, k)$ exactly like the parabola's vertex.

**Equations split in two**: $|x - 3| = 5$ means 'distance from 3 is 5' — so $x - 3 = 5$ or $x - 3 = -5$: $x = 8$ or $x = -2$. Isolate the absolute value FIRST, then split. And $|A| = -2$ has no solutions: distances are never negative.
**Inequalities**: $|x - 3| < 5$ traps $x$ within 5 of 3: $-5 < x - 3 < 5$, an AND sandwich. $|x - 3| > 5$ pushes $x$ beyond: $x - 3 > 5$ OR $x - 3 < -5$. Less-than: sandwich; greater-than: two tails.
lessons.keyIdea: Absolute value is distance: equations split into two branches after isolating, < makes an AND sandwich, > makes two OR tails, and |A| = negative is impossible.

facts.title: The split
facts.explanation: Isolate the bars first; then two branch equations.

facts.title: Sandwich vs tails
facts.latex: |X| < c: -c < X < c; \qquad |X| > c: X > c \text{ or } X < -c
facts.explanation: Within distance c = AND; beyond distance c = OR.

workedExamples.statement: A bolt is acceptable if its diameter $d$ satisfies $|d - 10| \le 0.2$ (mm). Solve the inequality and interpret.
workedExamples.solution: Sandwich: $-0.2 \le d - 10 \le 0.2$, so $9.8 \le d \le 10.2$. Any diameter from 9.8 mm to 10.2 mm — within 0.2 of the 10 mm target — passes.

commonMistakes.correction: The bars must stand ALONE first: $|x-1| = 4$, THEN split into $x - 1 = \pm 4$. Isolate, then branch.

commonMistakes.text: Solving $|x + 2| = -6$ by splitting into two equations anyway.
commonMistakes.correction: Stop at the sight of $= -6$: absolute value is a distance, never negative. No solutions — writing $x + 2 = -6$ manufactures a phantom.

tryIt.statement: Solve $|x - 7| > 2$ and describe the solution set.
tryIt.solution: Two tails: $x - 7 > 2 \to x > 9$, or $x - 7 < -2 \to x < 5$. Everything MORE than 2 away from 7: $x < 5$ or $x > 9$.

steps.title: Direction-blind numbers
steps.body: Tolerance specs, error bounds, 'within 3 of' — the real world constantly measures HOW FAR without caring WHICH WAY. Absolute value is that measurement, and because one distance corresponds to two positions, everything about it comes in pairs: two branches, two tails, two rays of a V.

steps.title: Distance from zero
steps.teach: Drag the marker: $|x|$ reads the DISTANCE back to 0, direction erased. $|-5|$ and $|5|$ both report 5 — two different positions, one distance. Every rule in this lesson falls out of this one picture.

steps.title: The V, transformed
steps.teach: Points of $y = |x - 3| - 2$: the corner sits at $(3, -2)$ — the transformation recipe from last lesson, verbatim. Rays of slope $+1$ and $-1$ climb away symmetrically; the V crosses zero at $x = 1$ and $x = 5$, each a distance 2 from the corner's $x$.

steps.title: Isolate, then split
steps.body: $|x - 3| = 5$ says: distance from 3 equals 5. Two positions do that: $3 + 5 = 8$ and $3 - 5 = -2$. Algebraically: $x - 3 = 5$ or $x - 3 = -5$. The non-negotiable ritual: get the bars ALONE first ($2|x-1| + 3 = 11 \to |x-1| = 4$), then write both branches. And if isolation leaves $|A| = $ negative: no solutions, full stop.

steps.title: Both positions
steps.explanation: Branches: $x + 2 = 7 \to x = 5$; $x + 2 = -7 \to x = -9$. Both sit distance 7 from $-2$.

steps.title: A negative distance?
steps.explanation: Isolating: $|3x - 5| = -5$. A distance can't be negative — no value of $x$ works. The split ritual never even starts.

steps.title: Sandwich or tails
steps.body: $|x - 3| < 5$: within 5 of 3 — one connected stretch, the AND sandwich $-5 < x - 3 < 5$, i.e. $-2 < x < 8$. $|x - 3| > 5$: beyond 5 from 3 — two separate tails, the OR pair $x > 8$ or $x < -2$. Less-than hugs the center; greater-than flees it. Picture the V against a horizontal line: below the line is the sandwich, above it the tails.

steps.title: Say it as distance
steps.body: Before any algebra, read the statement aloud as distance: '$|x - 7| > 2$' = 'x is more than 2 away from 7.' The number line answers instantly — beyond 9 or below 5 — and your algebra now has a picture to match against.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $|x|$ = distance from zero; the graph is a V with corner at $(h,k)$.
Isolate the bars, then split into two branches; $|A| = $ negative → no solutions.
$< c$: AND sandwich. $> c$: two OR tails.

lessons.title: Piecewise Functions
lessons.concreteComparison: A parking garage charges $3 for the first hour, then $2 per extra hour, capped at $15 — three different rules, each ruling its own time interval. Real pricing, taxes, and shipping are all stitched from pieces, and piecewise notation is just that stitching written down.
lessons.objective: Evaluate, graph, and write piecewise-defined functions, handling boundary points and open/closed endpoints correctly.
concept: A piecewise function lists rule + territory pairs: $f(x) = \begin{cases} x + 3 & x < 1 \\ x^2 & x \ge 1 \end{cases}$. To evaluate, FIRST find which territory the input lives in, THEN apply only that rule: $f(0) = 3$ (first rule), $f(2) = 4$ (second).
Boundaries belong to exactly one piece: at $x = 1$ the $\ge$ sends you to $x^2$, so $f(1) = 1$. Graphs mark this with a FILLED dot on the owning piece and an OPEN circle on the disowned one.
The absolute value function is secretly piecewise — $|x| = \begin{cases} x & x \ge 0 \\ -x & x < 0 \end{cases}$ — and step functions (shipping tiers, tax brackets) are piecewise with constant pieces.
lessons.keyIdea: One function, several territories: check which condition the input satisfies, apply only that rule, and give every boundary to exactly one piece (filled dot owns, open circle disowns).

facts.title: Evaluate in two steps
facts.explanation: $f(2)$: find the condition 2 satisfies, then use only that formula.

facts.title: Boundary ownership
facts.explanation: Each boundary input belongs to exactly one piece — the one whose inequality includes it.

workedExamples.statement: For $f(x) = \begin{cases} 2x + 5 & x < 0 \\ 5 - x & 0 \le x \le 4 \\ 1 & x > 4 \end{cases}$, compute $f(-3)$, $f(0)$, $f(4)$, and $f(10)$.
workedExamples.solution: $f(-3)$: territory $x < 0$ → $2(-3) + 5 = -1$. $f(0)$: middle territory (the $\le$ owns 0) → $5 - 0 = 5$. $f(4)$: middle still ($\le 4$) → $1$. $f(10)$: last → $1$.

workedExamples.statement: A taxi charges a $4 flag fee covering the first 2 km, then $1.50 per km after. Write the fare $C(d)$ as a piecewise function and price a 7 km ride.

commonMistakes.text: Evaluating every piece and reporting several outputs.
commonMistakes.correction: One input, one output — that's what makes it a function. Only the piece whose territory contains the input fires; the others don't exist for that $x$.

commonMistakes.text: Giving a boundary point to both pieces (two filled dots stacked).
commonMistakes.correction: Check the inequalities: exactly one includes the boundary ($\le$ or $\ge$); the strict one gets an open circle. Two filled dots at one $x$ fails the vertical line test.

tryIt.statement: For $g(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 & x \le 2 \\ 3x - 2 & x > 2 \end{cases}$, compute $g(-2)$, $g(2)$, and $g(5)$. Do the pieces meet at $x = 2$?
tryIt.solution: $g(-2) = 4$; $g(2) = 4$ (first piece owns 2); $g(5) = 13$. At $x = 2$ the second rule would give $3(2) - 2 = 4$ too — the pieces MEET: the graph is connected there.

tryIt.statement: Cell data costs $10 for up to 5 GB, then $2 per extra GB. Write $P(g)$ and price 9 GB.

steps.title: When one rule isn't enough
steps.body: Your parking garage, your tax bracket, your shipping fee — none obey a single formula. They switch rules at thresholds. Piecewise notation stitches several rules into ONE function by assigning each rule a territory. The skill: never let two rules fire at once.

steps.title: A stitched graph
steps.teach: Points from $f(x) = x + 3$ for $x < 1$ stitched to $f(x) = x^2$ for $x \ge 1$: the line climbs to the seam at $x = 1$, where an open circle at height 4 hands off to a filled dot at height 1 — the parabola piece owns the boundary ($\ge$). One x, one owner.

steps.title: Territory first, rule second
steps.body: $f(2)$: don't touch any formula yet. STEP 1: which condition does 2 satisfy? STEP 2: apply that rule only. Discipline matters at the seams — $f(1)$ with conditions $x < 1$ and $x \ge 1$ goes to the second piece, because $\ge$ includes 1 and $<$ does not. Exactly one piece owns each input.

steps.title: Who owns 3?
steps.explanation: The $\ge$ owns 3: $f(3) = 2(3) = 6$. The first piece stops strictly before 3 — open circle at height 4, filled dot at height 6.

steps.title: From story to braces
steps.body: Real stories hand you the pieces: a flat fee UP TO some threshold, a per-unit rate BEYOND it. Translate each clause into rule + territory, and make the boundary belong to exactly one clause ('up to 2 km' → $d \le 2$). Then test the seam: does a 2 km ride cost the flag fee under both readings? It should.

steps.title: Read the tiers
steps.prompt: Shipping: $\$5$ for orders under $\$50$, free otherwise. Which is the correct piecewise cost?
steps.explanation: 'Under \$50' is strict ($<$); 'otherwise' takes everything else ($\ge$), including the boundary \$50 exactly — which ships free. Option B double-books $o = 50$.

steps.title: Audit the seams
steps.body: Every piecewise error lives at a boundary. After writing or reading one, evaluate AT each seam: exactly one rule should claim it, and if the pieces happen to agree there (like $x^2$ meeting $3x - 2$ at $x = 2$), the graph is connected — worth noticing, since later mathematics (continuity) is built on exactly this check.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Territory first, rule second — one piece fires per input.
Each boundary has exactly one owner: filled dot owns, open circle disowns.
Stories with thresholds (fees, tiers, brackets) ARE piecewise functions.

practice.solution: Denominator zero at $x = \pm 4$: all reals except $4$ and $-4$.
Corner of the recipe: left 1, down 9 — vertex $(-1, -9)$.
Right 6, up 2, flipped over the x-axis: an upside-down V with corner $(6, 2)$.

practice.statement: For $f(x) = \begin{cases} 5 - 2x & x \le 1 \\ x^2 + 2 & x > 1 \end{cases}$, compute $f(1)$ and $f(3)$.

testYourself.statement: The graph of $y = \sqrt{x}$ is shifted left 4, stretched vertically by 3, and shifted down 6. Write the equation and compute its value at $x = 0$.
A gym charges $30/month for up to 10 visits, plus $4 per extra visit. Write $C(v)$ and price a 16-visit month.
